127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)

5504	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
5505	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
5506	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
5507	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
5508	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
5509	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
5510	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
5511	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
5512	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
5513	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
5514	
5515	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
5516	  definitional equality, and --- crucially --- that the dual is \emph{not sectionwise}.
5517	  Unlike the tensor product, whose value is computed fibrewise
5518	  (\((M \otimes N)(V) = M(V) \otimes N(V)\)), the dual evaluates to a Hom over an entire
5519	  down-set: for an open \(V \subseteq U\),
5520	  \[
5521	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)(V)
5522	      \;=\; \mathcal{H}om\bigl(M|_V,\ \mathcal{O}|_V\bigr)\big|_{\mathtt{Over}\,V},
5523	  \]
5524	  a module of morphisms of presheaves over the whole slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq
5525	  \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\), \emph{not} a single fibre \(M(V) \to \mathcal{O}(V)\). Consequently
5526	  the assertion that restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes with the dual does
5527	  \emph{not} reduce to a single ground-ring reconciliation. The proof proceeds in two
5528	  stages: a preliminary adjunction-uniqueness rewrite (stage~H1) that re-presents the
5529	  pulled-back dual in pushforward form, followed by the resolution of the resulting
5530	  \emph{residual} into \emph{two} legs, carried out objectwise in the open \(V\) (with
5531	  \(V \subseteq U\)) and assembled over the slice with poset-thin naturality.
5532	
5533	  \emph{Stage H1 --- adjunction-uniqueness rewrite.} The pulled-back dual is initially
5534	  presented in the \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)-form inherited from the abstract
5535	  definition of the restriction functor, and no sectionwise comparison can begin against
5536	  that opaque form. We first rewrite it into a \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\)-form: the
5537	  restriction-along-an-open-immersion pullback and the pushforward along the structure-ring
5538	  map \(\beta\) are both left adjoints to one and the same functor, so by uniqueness of left
5539	  adjoints --- the adjunction \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) composed with
5540	  \(\mathtt{leftAdjointUniq}\) --- they are canonically isomorphic, and this isomorphism
5541	  transports the pulled-back dual into pushforward form. After H1 the residual obligation is
5542	  \emph{exactly}
5543	  \[
5544	    (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,(\mathtt{dual}\,M.\mathtt{val})
5545	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
5546	    \mathtt{dual}\bigl((\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathtt{obj}\,M.\mathtt{val}\bigr),
5547	  \]
5548	  i.e.\ ``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual''. It is this residual ---
5549	  \emph{not} the original Step-4 isomorphism --- that legs (A) and (B) below resolve; legs
5550	  (A) and (B) are the content remaining \emph{after} the H1 rewrite, not a standalone
5551	  two-leg split of the whole step.
5552	
5553	  \emph{Leg (A) --- slice-site Hom base-change (Beck--Chevalley).} The domain presheaf must
5554	  first be transported across the open immersion. Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
5555	  direct-image functor on open sets is fully faithful with image
5556	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); this carries the restricted domain
5557	  \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,V \subseteq \mathrm{Opens}\,Y\) to the
5558	  restricted domain \(\mathtt{restr}_{fV}\,M\) over \(\mathtt{Over}\,(fV) \subseteq
5559	  \mathrm{Opens}\,X\), and hence identifies the two Hom-modules computed over these matched
5560	  slices. This is a genuine slice-site Hom base-change --- a Beck--Chevalley square on the
5561	  internal hom across the fully-faithful open-immersion functor, restricted to the down-set
5562	  of \(fV\) --- \emph{not} a fibrewise identity.
5563	
5564	  \emph{Leg (B) --- ground-ring reconciliation.} The codomain of the internal hom is in
5565	  both cases the structure sheaf. Once leg (A) has matched the domains, the unit codomain
5566	  is reconciled along the open-immersion ring isomorphism
5567	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}_V \colon
5568	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\): restriction of scalars along the
5569	  ring \emph{isomorphism} \(\beta_V\) is an equivalence, and its ModuleCat-level
5570	  shadow on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}, carrying the
5571	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
5572	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly. This is the dual analogue, at the module level, of the
5573	  ring-isomorphism tensor equivalence (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}).
5574	
5575	  The residual of stage~H1 (``\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) commutes with the dual'') is
5576	  the sectionwise composite of leg (A) followed by leg (B), packaged over the slice with
5577	  poset-thin naturality; the full Step-4 isomorphism is then the H1 isomorphism followed by
5578	  this composite. The slice-reindexing coherence is \emph{light}, because \(\mathrm{Opens}\)
5579	  is a thin poset and the relevant squares commute by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\),
5580	  whereas H1 and legs (A) and (B) carry the substantive content.
5581	
5582	  It is worth recording \emph{why} the strong-monoidal restriction
5583	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}) has no direct dual analogue here. For
5584	  the tensor product the transport \emph{collapsed to leg (B) alone}: because \(\otimes\)
5585	  is sectionwise, pushing a tensor forward reduced, section by section, to a single
5586	  ground-ring tensor equivalence, with no domain slice left to re-index. The dual's
5587	  non-sectionwise nature removes that collapse and forces the additional leg (A): the
5588	  domain \(\mathtt{restr}_V(f_*M)\) genuinely lives over a varying slice and must be
5589	  transported before the ground ring can be reconciled.
5590	
5591	  We caution that the naive ``fixed-value-category slice equivalence'' of
5592	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} is \emph{not} applicable to leg (A). That instrument
5593	  operates in a fixed value category over a fixed base ring, whereas the residual of
5594	  leg (A) lives at the \emph{presheaf} level, with a per-section ring \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)
5595	  that varies with \(V\) and a finer slicing than the sheaf-level equivalence resolves;
5596	  leg (A) must therefore be built directly at the presheaf-of-modules / varying-ring level.
5597	
5598	  Composing legs (A) and (B) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
5599	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values; assembling these into a
5600	  presheaf-of-modules natural isomorphism and sheafifying through the sheaf-level dual of
5601	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} resolves the H1 residual. Precomposing with the stage-H1
5602	  rewrite then yields the stated isomorphism
5603	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f \cong \mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\).
5604	
5605	  \emph{An alternative route}, not committed to here, derives the isomorphism from the
5606	  already-established \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} by uniqueness of monoidal inverses:
5607	  the dual is the \(\otimes\)-inverse of an invertible module and restriction commutes with
5608	  \(\otimes\), so evaluation/coevaluation naturality transports the inverse. This sidesteps
5609	  leg (A) entirely, and is a candidate should the inverse-uniqueness glue prove cheaper than
5610	  building the slice-site base-change directly.
5611	\end{proof}

5618	  Let \(Y\) be a scheme. The sheaf-level dual of the structure sheaf is canonically the
5619	  structure sheaf itself,
5620	  \[

5622	  \]

5626	  at \(1\) identifies the internal hom of the unit into itself with the unit.
5627	\end{lemma}

8	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
9	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
10	
11	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
12	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
13	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
14	\[
15	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
16	  \qquad
17	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
18	\]
19	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
20	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
21	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
22	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
23	
24	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
25	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
26	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
27	% groups)
28	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
29	% SOURCE QUOTE:
30	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
31	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
32	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
33	% abelian groups defined by the formula
34	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
35	% \end{dfn}
36	%  [\dots]
37	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
38	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
40	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
41	% topologies  by
42	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
43	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
44	%  \end{dfn}"
45	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
46	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
47	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
48	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
49	homomorphisms.}
50	
51	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
52	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
53	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
54	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
55	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
56	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
57	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
58	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
59	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
60	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
61	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
62	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
63	structure. This is the content of
64	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
65	
66	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
67	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
68	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
69	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
70	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
71	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
72	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
73	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
74	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
75	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
76	\begin{enumerate}
77	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
78	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
79	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
80	    isomorphism classes;
81	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
82	    for \(\otimes\);
83	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
84	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
85	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
86	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
87	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
88	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
89	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
90	\end{enumerate}
91	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
92	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
93	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
94	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
95	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
96	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
97	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
98	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
99	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
100	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
101	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
102	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
103	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
104	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
105	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
106	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
107	supplied.
108	
109	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
110	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
111	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
112	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
113	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
114	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
115	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
116	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
118	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
119	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
121	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
122	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
123	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
124	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
125	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
126	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
127	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
128	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
129	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
130	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it

% NOTE: D3' is the SOLE genuinely-new sub-step of the loc-triv route. It asserts the
% GENERAL composition coherence of the pullback tensorator delta for ANY composable
% scheme morphisms h : Z -> Y, f : Y -> X (the comparison for the composite factors
% through the comparisons for f and h, conjugated by the pullback pseudofunctoriality
% iso pullbackComp h f). The open-immersion base-change square consumed by D4' is the
% specialisation h := j'. pullbackTensorMap is NOT an adjunction transpose (it is a
% hand-built four-fold composite), so the unit-analog mate calculus of
% lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp does NOT transfer; the genuine route is the four-square
% comp_delta build described in the proof.
\begin{lemma}

\leanok
[Composition coherence of the pullback tensorator $\delta$ (D3$'$)]
  \label{lem:pullback_tensor_map_basechange}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullbackTensorMap_restrict}
  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural,
        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  Let \(h : Z \to Y\) and \(f : Y \to X\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Write \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} =
  \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) for the sheaf-level pullback--tensor comparison of
  \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}, \(h^{*}(-) = (\mathtt{pullback}\,h).\mathrm{map}\) for
  the pullback functor, and \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\) for the pullback
  pseudofunctoriality isomorphism \((h \circ f)^{*} \cong h^{*}\circ f^{*}\). Then the
  comparison for the composite \(h \circ f\) factors through the comparisons for \(f\)
  and for \(h\), conjugated by \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\):
  \[
    \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,h \circ f}(M, N)
      \;=\;
      (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f).\mathrm{inv}_{M \otimes_X N} \;\mathbin{;}\;
      h^{*}\!\bigl(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,f}(M, N)\bigr) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      \delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}}^{\,h}(f^{*}M,\, f^{*}N) \;\mathbin{;}\;
      \mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M,\,
        (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_N\bigr).\mathrm{hom},
  \]
  an equality of morphisms \((h \circ f)^{*}(M \otimes_X N) \to (h^{*}f^{*}M)
  \otimes_Z (h^{*}f^{*}N)\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,Z\), where
  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\) is the functoriality of \(\otimes\) in a pair of
  isomorphisms.

  \emph{Remark (the open-immersion base-change square).} The base-change form consumed
  downstream by \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv} is the \emph{specialisation} of
  this coherence. Given \(f : Y \to X\), an open \(U \subseteq X\) with preimage
  \(f^{-1}U\), the restriction \(g : f^{-1}U \to U\) and the open immersions
  \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\), \(j' : f^{-1}U \hookrightarrow Y\), the morphism
  \(f^{-1}U \to X\) admits the two equal factorisations \(j' \circ f = g \circ j\)
  (i.e. \(j' \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} j\)). Applying the displayed coherence to
  each factorisation and equating the two results identifies the restriction
  \((j')^{*}\bigl(\delta^{f}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(M,N)\bigr)\) with
  \(\delta^{g}_{\mathrm{sheaf}}(j^{*}M, j^{*}N)\), through the pseudofunctoriality
  isomorphisms; in particular each is an isomorphism if and only if the other is. This
  is the form used in the chart-chase, where \(M, N\) are trivialised on \(U\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:pullback_tensor_map, lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural,
        lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
  Unfolding \(\delta_{\mathrm{sheaf}} = \mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\)
  (\cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map}) on both sides exposes each as the same four-fold
  composite
  \[
    S_1 \;\mathbin{;}\; a.\mathrm{map}\,\delta \;\mathbin{;}\; S_3 \;\mathbin{;}\; S_4,
  \]
  where \(S_1\) is the sheafification--pullback comparison
  \(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\), \(a.\mathrm{map}\,\delta\) is the
  sheafification of the presheaf-level oplax comparison \(\delta\) of
  \cref{lem:presheaf_pullback_oplaxmonoidal}, \(S_3\) is the monoidal-unit
  sheafification isomorphism \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\), and \(S_4\) is the
  tensor of the connecting isomorphisms \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\). The identity to
  prove is therefore a paste of \emph{four commuting squares}, conjugated throughout by
  the pullback pseudofunctoriality isomorphism \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f\). Unlike
  the \emph{naturality} paste of \cref{lem:pullback_tensor_map_natural} (D1$'$), this is
  a \emph{composition}-coherence paste: the left-hand side is the composite-morphism
  (\(h \circ f\)) instance, while the right-hand side interleaves the \(f\)-instance
  (transported by \(h^{*}\)) with the \(h\)-instance (on the pulled-back modules
  \(f^{*}M, f^{*}N\)).

  We record \emph{why} the unit-comparison mirror does not apply. The unit coherence
  \cref{lem:pullbackObjUnitToUnit_comp} succeeds because
  \(\mathtt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit}\) is, by definition, an adjunction transpose, so its
  composition coherence is obtained by transposing across the pullback--pushforward
  adjunction. The tensorator \(\mathtt{pullbackTensorMap}\) is \emph{not} a transpose ---
  it is the hand-built four-fold composite above --- and there is no analogous
  \(\mathtt{homEquiv}\)-bridge, so the transpose-and-inject opening of the unit analog
  leaves an un-evaluable transpose of a concrete composite and stalls. The genuine route
  pastes the four squares directly.

  \emph{Sq2 (the \(\delta\) core).} The presheaf-level oplax comparison \(\delta\) of the
  \emph{composite} pullback decomposes by the Mathlib oplax-monoidal coherence
  \(\mathtt{Functor.OplaxMonoidal.comp\_}\delta\), once the composite presheaf pullback
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h \circ f}\) is identified with the composite functor
  \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{f} \,\circ\, \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi'_{h}\) through the
  Mathlib presheaf coherence \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp}\). This
  identification has a genuine prerequisite: the ring-map reconciliation
  \[
    (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,(h \circ f)).\mathrm{hom}
      \;=\; \varphi'_{f} \;\mathbin{;}\;
      (\mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base})^{\mathrm{op}}.\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\,\varphi'_{h},
  \]
  which is non-trivial because the two sides live in functor categories that agree only
  up to the equality \(\mathtt{Opens.map}\,(h.\mathrm{base} \circ f.\mathrm{base}) =
  \mathtt{Opens.map}\,f.\mathrm{base} \,\circ\, \mathtt{Opens.map}\,h.\mathrm{base}\)
  (\(\mathtt{Opens.map\_comp}\)), so the reconciliation is transported by the
  pseudofunctor coherence. The bookkeeping atoms
  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.}\{\mathtt{pullbackId}, \mathtt{pullback\_assoc},
  \mathtt{pullback\_comp\_id}, \mathtt{pullback\_id\_comp}\}\) discharge the residual
  associativity and unit squares.

  \emph{Sq1 (sheafification \(\leftrightarrow\) pullback).} The composition coherence of
  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\) across \(h \circ f\) --- the
  analog of \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for the ``sheafify-then-pullback'' natural
  isomorphism --- is \emph{absent from Mathlib} and is supplied as a standalone project
  sub-lemma (mate-calculus style).

  \emph{Sq3 (unit-iso transport).} The monoidal-unit sheafification isomorphism
  \(\mathtt{sheafifyTensorUnitIso}\) is carried through the same \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\)
  identification used for Sq2.

  \emph{Sq4 (the connecting iso).} The scheme-level composition coherence of
  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\) relates \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,(h \circ f)\,M\) to
  \(h^{*}\bigl(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,f\,M\bigr)\),
  \(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\,h\,(f^{*}M)\), and the pseudofunctoriality component
  \((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M\); it produces the final
  \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoOfIso}\bigl((\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_M,
  (\mathtt{pullbackComp}\,h\,f)_N\bigr)\) factor. This coherence is likewise \emph{absent
  from Mathlib} and is the second standalone project sub-lemma.

  The two genuinely missing ingredients are therefore the composition coherences Sq1
  (\(\mathtt{sheafificationCompPullback}\)) and Sq4 (\(\mathtt{pullbackValIso}\)),
  together with the Sq2 ring-map reconciliation; the adjunction-mate bridge
  \(\mathtt{conjugateEquiv\_pullbackComp\_inv}\) (relating \(\mathtt{pullbackComp}\) for
  the left adjoints to \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) for the right adjoints) is the device
  used inside Sq2 and Sq4 to transport the pseudofunctoriality coherence across the
  adjunctions. With Sq1 and Sq4 in hand, the Sq2 oplax decomposition and the Sq3 unit-iso
  transport assemble the four squares into the displayed identity. The base-change-square
  consequence (the Remark) is then the specialisation \(h := j'\) obtained by equating the
  two factorisations \(j' \mathbin{;} f = g \mathbin{;} j\); the iff-clause is immediate,
  an isomorphism being preserved and reflected by the pseudofunctoriality isomorphism it
  is conjugated by.
\end{proof}
